Meaning: Used to indicate immediate sequence between events.
Examples: As soon as the rain stopped, the runners started again. | Please call me as soon as you finish booking the tickets. | Send the receipt as soon as the payment clears.

Front: In order to close the report, please attach the attendance sheet.
Meaning: Used to introduce a purpose, often before an infinitive.
Examples: He stayed quiet in order to keep the surprise secret. | The team postponed shipping in order to fix the security issue. | We changed the route in order to avoid the flooded road.

Front: At the moment, our office is using a rotating weekend schedule.
Meaning: Used to describe the current time period or present situation.
Examples: At the moment, the building is closed for renovation. | The team is still using temporary desks at the moment. | At the moment, I cannot confirm the final price.

Front: In the meantime, the volunteers prepared the welcome packets.
Meaning: Used to describe what happens while waiting for something else.
Examples: She booked a hotel for next week; in the meantime, she set up a video chat. | The storm warning is active, so we will wait at home in the meantime. | The new laptop ships tomorrow; in the meantime, use the shared machine.

Front: In the process of migrating the database, we found two outdated scripts.
Meaning: Used when an action is currently underway.
Examples: The school is in the process of rewiring the science lab. | We are in the process of reviewing all expense receipts. | They found three errors in the process of checking the archive.

Front: At the same time, she kept the meeting focused and encouraged everyone to speak.
Meaning: Used to show two things happen together.
Examples: The app is easy to use at the same time as it remains secure. | The design looks modern, and at the same time, it is affordable. | She wanted speed and, at the same time, refused to skip testing.

Front: On the other hand, that route saves money but takes longer.
Meaning: Used to present a contrasting point.
Examples: The upgrade is expensive; on the other hand, it may reduce maintenance for years. | The cafe is small, but on the other hand, the atmosphere is excellent. | The cheaper hotel is farther away; on the other hand, breakfast is included.

Front: As a result of the delay, the launch date moved to Friday.
Meaning: Used to show a consequence of a previous cause.
Examples: She forgot the charger, and as a result, she missed the first online class. | The leak was fixed early, and as a result, data loss stopped. | The form was incomplete; as a result, approval took another week.

Front: In addition, we added a backup key to the account setup.
Meaning: Used to add extra information.
Examples: The contract includes training. In addition, support is available 24/7. | She bought two books, and in addition, she ordered a desk lamp. | In addition, every invoice must include a project code.

Front: On the contrary, the low bid did not include any hidden fees.
Meaning: Used to introduce the opposite of a previous idea.
Examples: The room looked quiet; on the contrary, it was packed with volunteers. | Some think the project is slow, on the contrary, the code quality has improved. | The tool is not slower; on the contrary, it cuts loading time in half.

Front: In contrast, older models were slower but more durable.
Meaning: Used to show a clear difference between two things.
Examples: City traffic is heavy; in contrast, the suburban roads were empty. | The first draft was vague, and in contrast, the revision is very specific. | In contrast, the second group improved after only two lessons.

Front: In the first place, the team should confirm the timezone.
Meaning: Used to emphasize the original reason or starting point.
Examples: I asked for the raw file in the first place, then I requested the summary. | The policy was unrealistic in the first place, so we redesigned it. | Why was the temporary file shared in the first place?

Front: In the long run, this library will cut support costs.
Meaning: Used to describe long-term outcomes.
Examples: The startup invested in documentation; in the long run it reduced onboarding time. | The patient improved slowly, and in the long run the treatment worked well. | In the long run, clear documentation saves more time than quick fixes.

Front: In the short term, we will keep one support agent on call.
Meaning: Used to describe near-future or temporary effects.
Examples: The company cut overtime this quarter, and in the short term output dipped. | She accepted a temporary role in the short term to cover a leave. | In the short term, the manual workaround is acceptable.

Phrase: at the end of the day
Front: At the end of the day, reputation mattered more than features.
Meaning: Used to return to the main conclusion.
Examples: The speakers praised many options, but at the end of the day, reliability won. | At the end of the day, the team agreed to keep the manual review step. | At the end of the day, the customer needs a working product.

Front: In broad terms, the strategy was to reduce waste and add clarity.
Meaning: Used to state a general idea without fine detail.
Examples: In broad terms, remote work changed how the team communicates. | The law affects many sectors, in broad terms mostly food and healthcare. | In broad terms, the policy rewards speed but punishes risk-taking.

Front: In practical terms, the app needs an offline fallback.
Meaning: Used to translate an idea into concrete, real-world effect.
Examples: In practical terms, that means fewer clicks for each report. | The theory sounds fine, but in practical terms the team needed stronger training. | In practical terms, the change means fewer evening shifts.

Front: In detail, the audit documented every network path change.
Meaning: Used to describe something fully and specifically.
Examples: She explained the model in detail so the committee could approve it. | The manual walks through the process in detail, step by step. | The appendix explains the calculation in detail.

Front: For the time being, the hallway will be open only on weekdays.
Meaning: Used to refer to a temporary arrangement.
Examples: The event is paused; for the time being, registration is manual. | They will lease the studio for the time being while they look for a permanent space. | For the time being, keep using the old login page.

Front: At first glance, the report looked complete.
Meaning: Used to refer to an initial, quick impression.
Examples: At first glance the painting looked modern, but it was a classic replica. | The bug looked minor at first glance; deeper tests found three causes. | At first glance, the proposal looked expensive but realistic.

Front: As far as the map shows, the route is two hours longer.
Meaning: Used to indicate extent or limit of knowledge.
Examples: As far as our tests show, the fix is stable. | As far as our team can tell, the outage was caused by power loss. | As far as I know, no one has approved the final design.

Front: As long as you confirm the code, the build will start automatically.
Meaning: Used to state a condition for another action.
Examples: You can stay as long as you finish this task first. | As long as we have power backup, the server will remain online. | You can borrow the laptop as long as you return it tonight.

Front: She helped as much as she could before leaving for the night.
Meaning: Used to compare amount, degree, or intensity.
Examples: He values speed as much as reliability in tools. | I can stay as much as you need, especially during the launch week. | As much as I like the idea, we cannot afford it this month.

Front: As well as the core package, the plugin handles exports.
Meaning: Used to add one item in addition to another.
Examples: The room has heating as well as central cooling. | She speaks Spanish as well as French for international clients. | The course covers pronunciation as well as grammar.

Front: As soon as possible, send the invoice to the finance team.
Meaning: Used to request action at the earliest feasible time.
Examples: The technician should arrive as soon as possible after the outage. | Send the replacement part as soon as possible, but only after powering down. | Please send the signed copy as soon as possible.

Front: As often as the weather is calm, we hike by the lake.
Meaning: Used to describe frequency of repeated events.
Examples: She calls her mother as often as she can. | We review the design as often as every Friday before release. | Check the dashboard as often as the data changes.

Front: At any rate, we still have a backup plan in case the train is late.
Meaning: Used to state a fallback minimum or fallback perspective.
Examples: I don't know the full budget, but at any rate the proposal is within range. | At any rate, the team approved the safer option. | At any rate, we should keep a backup copy.

Front: At least, we confirmed the date before posting the invite.
Meaning: Used to note the minimum amount, limit, or expectation.
Examples: At least three sensors are required for this model. | The room is on the second floor, at least according to the building map. | At least one reviewer should check the final version.

Front: Try the espresso at least once before comparing the blends.
Meaning: Used to indicate that something should happen a minimum single time.
Examples: I have watched that documentary at least once, and it still changes my perspective. | The team should review the checklist at least once after every shift. | Every new hire should shadow support at least once.

Front: At your convenience, please upload the signed agreement.
Meaning: Used to say someone may do something when they have time.
Examples: She sent the photos at your convenience and later asked if she should post them. | At your convenience, we can reschedule the interview. | At your convenience, send me the revised travel dates.

Front: By the way, the keynote starts at 4 p.m., not 3.
Meaning: Used to add a side note or related point.
Examples: The package shipped today; by the way, it includes the charger too. | He asked about shipping. By the way, did you check the serial number? | By the way, the Spanish deck update finished successfully.

Front: By then, the committee will have reviewed all applications.
Meaning: Used to refer to a specific future point in time.
Examples: The office should be repainted by then, and the signs will be updated. | We expect the update to finish by then, so we can demo early next week. | By then, the new cards should already be in Anki.

Front: By all means, invite the entire lab to the welcome meeting.
Meaning: Used for enthusiastic permission or strong encouragement.
Examples: They were worried about the pitch, and by all means, she pushed for a public trial. | If you are free on Tuesday, by all means take the lead. | If the wording is unclear, by all means rewrite it.

Front: By and large, customers preferred the faster interface.
Meaning: Used to give a general overall judgment.
Examples: By and large, the program increased engagement across all regions. | The city is expensive, and by and large, tourists still visit in summer. | By and large, the feedback was positive.

Front: From now on, we will track each shipment with a barcode.
Meaning: Used to indicate a change starting from the present.
Examples: From now on, no invoices are sent without receipts. | The school will lock after 8 p.m. from now on. | From now on, every card needs a stable source ID.

Front: From time to time, the train skips a stop for maintenance.
Meaning: Used for occasional or intermittent events.
Examples: We host volunteer days from time to time during the winter. | Their server gets audited from time to time to catch hidden risks. | From time to time, older examples need another review.

Front: She built the prototype from scratch in three weeks.
Meaning: Used when creating something with no prior base.
Examples: He learned the language from scratch by coding every evening. | We rewrote the website from scratch after the migration failed. | We rebuilt the import script from scratch after the format changed.

Front: From the start, the team logged every decision in a shared note.
Meaning: Used to refer to the beginning of a process.
Examples: From the start, we told participants there would be no penalties for late questions. | The project was documented from the start, which avoided confusion later. | From the start, the deck was meant for recognition practice.

Front: From the beginning, the lesson used real examples and short quizzes.
Meaning: Used to refer to the starting point of something.
Examples: We explained the policy from the beginning, so no one was surprised later. | The book was written from the beginning as a practical guide. | Read the instructions from the beginning before editing the file.

Front: From the outset, she insisted on encrypted backups.
Meaning: Used to indicate conditions fixed at the earliest stage.
Examples: From the outset, the museum planned a family-friendly route. | The campaign team set accessibility goals from the outset. | From the outset, the plan required safe Anki updates.

Front: For the record, the meeting started at 10:05 with three attendees.
Meaning: Used to make a statement formally recorded.
Examples: For the record, the supplier confirmed delivery before noon. | She asked for silence for the record, then read the announcement. | For the record, the deck now has 360 cards.

Front: For practical reasons, we kept the meeting in the smaller room.
Meaning: Used to justify a choice based on real-world practicality.
Examples: They moved data to a local cache for practical reasons, not theory. | For practical reasons, we switched vendors before launch. | For practical reasons, we kept the note type simple.

Front: For all intents and purposes, the pilot is fully operational.
Meaning: Equivalent phrase meaning "practically true in all relevant ways."
Examples: The archive is locked; for all intents and purposes, it is secure. | The bridge is ready for all intents and purposes, though inspectors still finish paperwork. | For all intents and purposes, the preview deck became the real deck.

Front: For this reason, the team moved the backup server out of the office.
Meaning: Used to introduce a specific reason for what follows.
Examples: The storm flooded the lower floor; for this reason, we closed the gallery. | Her visa was delayed, and for this reason the trip was postponed. | For this reason, I checked the import file after rendering.

Front: For your safety, the train doors will close automatically during the station stop.
Meaning: Used to explain measures taken to protect someone.
Examples: Keep your badge visible for your safety in the restricted lab. | We lowered the speed limits for your safety in the parking area. | For your safety, do not enter the lab without goggles.

Front: To begin with, we tested the core workflow and only then added extras.
Meaning: Used to open an explanation with first point.
Examples: To begin with, please read the user guide, then submit feedback. | The chef prepared the sauce to begin with, and only then plated the dish. | To begin with, review the high-frequency phrases.

Front: To be frank, the launch felt rushed and underprepared.
Meaning: Used to introduce an honest or direct opinion.
Examples: To be frank, I did not expect the room to fill up so quickly. | The memo was vague; to be frank, everyone wanted the deadline moved. | To be frank, the first generated examples were too repetitive.

Front: To sum up, the proposal passes if we reduce support costs by ten percent.
Meaning: Used to summarize a discussion or section.
Examples: The data was clear; to sum up, we need a redesign. | To sum up, safety checks and user testing are non-negotiable. | To sum up, the structure is good but examples need review.

Phrase: to make a long story short
Front: To make a long story short, we lost one supplier and recovered quickly.
Meaning: Used to give a brief summary after details.
Examples: The route was delayed, detoured, and rerouted; to make a long story short, everyone arrived late. | We can explain all the issues later; to make a long story short, the build is stable. | To make a long story short, the update worked.

Front: To be clear, any edits must be approved by design.
Meaning: Used to state meaning plainly before giving details.
Examples: To be clear, I will only commit after review. | The contract says this is optional, and to be clear, no fees are listed. | To be clear, no existing cards were deleted.

Front: To tell the truth, the app needed one more round of testing.
Meaning: Used to signal a candid disclosure.
Examples: The plan looked chaotic; to tell the truth, we had missed a major assumption. | She praised the design, and to tell the truth, preferred the simpler layout. | To tell the truth, I prefer fewer but better cards.

Front: It was inconvenient, to say the least, during the power outage.
Meaning: Used to indicate minimum characterization, often with understatement.
Examples: The ride was delayed, to say the least, by a storm. | The result was disappointing, to say the least, compared with last quarter. | The first draft was uneven, to say the least.

Front: To a large extent, team morale improved after the office redesign.
Meaning: Used to indicate a strong degree though not complete.
Examples: The recommendation is reliable to a large extent, though not perfect. | The delay was due to one issue; to a large extent, it came from the old router. | To a large extent, progress depends on daily reviews.

Front: She was to the point in every status update.
Meaning: Used to keep discussion focused and concise.
Examples: The manager gave a to the point summary during the five-minute check-in. | His feedback was short and to the point. | Keep your answer short and to the point.

Front: To the contrary, every report showed improved engagement.
Meaning: Used to reject a previous statement and present opposite evidence.
Examples: People expected delays; to the contrary, the launch was early. | The software looked risky, yet to the contrary, users reported fewer bugs. | There is no evidence to the contrary in the logs.

Front: In terms of speed, the new model is much better.
Meaning: Used to define scope or measurement using a specific criterion.
Examples: In terms of security, this tool is still untested. | In terms of cost, the option is manageable, but staffing is still a challenge. | In terms of usefulness, examples matter more than definitions.

Front: In relation to budget, we can still add one more week.
Meaning: Used to compare or connect a topic to another.
Examples: The bonus policy, in relation to performance, is straightforward. | In relation to customer feedback, the dashboard changes look successful. | In relation to grammar, phrases often show real usage.

Front: Step back and look at it in the bigger picture before deciding which feature to ship next.
Meaning: Used to evaluate a situation by considering all its parts together.
Examples: The team reviewed budget, timeline, and risk in the bigger picture before changing scope. | When the store missed its quarterly target, we looked at sales in the bigger picture, not one bad week. | He rejected the shortcut because in the bigger picture it hurt customer trust.

Front: She tracks expenses on a regular basis so the team can catch spending spikes early.
Meaning: Done repeatedly at consistent intervals, not just occasionally.
Examples: The clinic sends test results on a regular basis to all patients. | We exercise on a regular basis to avoid burnout during exam week. | The warehouse audits stock on a regular basis before seasonal shipping starts.

Front: By the time we launched, everyone was on the same page about the release timeline.
Meaning: Having the same understanding and agreement about the current plan.
Examples: The manager made sure legal and engineering were on the same page before publishing the announcement. | After a few clarifying calls, we all got on the same page about who handled customer support. | The family ended their debate once they were on the same page about the budget.

Front: Can you explain the whole contract in a nutshell so I can brief my director?
Meaning: Summarizing a topic very briefly and clearly.
Examples: Her report put three months of feedback in a nutshell at the end of the meeting. | If you want in a nutshell, the issue is a broken payment webhook. | He gave the policy update in a nutshell before the team shifted to implementation details.

Front: In the final analysis, the data showed the design improved conversions enough to keep.
Meaning: After all other points are considered, this is the ultimate conclusion.
Examples: In the final analysis, delayed approvals cost us a full week. | The court reviewed every witness first, and in the final analysis, the evidence was clear. | In the final analysis, we kept the simpler option even though it seemed less ambitious.

Front: They drove for hours and arrived in the middle of nowhere with no signal.
Meaning: An extremely remote or isolated place with little nearby activity.
Examples: The repair crew was stuck in the middle of nowhere when the bridge closed. | She loves visiting a cabin in the middle of nowhere during winter breaks. | The festival was cancelled because the stage was in the middle of nowhere far from help.

Front: The presenter praised the idea and in the same breath warned about budget risks.
Meaning: Used to connect two linked ideas in one statement.
Examples: She mentioned the delay and in the same breath offered an apology. | He accepted the bonus in the same breath as admitting the team had missed two deadlines. | I can't promise a discount in the same breath as guaranteeing same-day delivery.

Front: Could you put that in plain terms so the client can approve it quickly?
Meaning: Explained in straightforward, easy-to-understand language.
Examples: The engineer rephrased the proposal in plain terms for nontechnical stakeholders. | The doctor gave the treatment plan in plain terms before asking for consent. | When the memo came out, HR rewrote it in plain terms for new hires.

Front: The board asked for the vision in concrete terms, with dates and owners.
Meaning: Expressed in specific, practical details rather than abstractions.
Examples: Our timeline is clear in concrete terms: draft by Monday, review Tuesday, publish Friday. | The architect described the concept in concrete terms so we could estimate costs. | The policy sounded idealistic until we translated it in concrete terms.

Front: Accepting the settlement may be in your best interest if the trial would take years.
Meaning: Doing something because it benefits you most in the long run.
Examples: The legal counsel advised settlement in your best interest after the long trial. | The pharmacist explained two treatment plans in your best interest. | The bank manager chose a lower-fee card in your best interest, even though commissions were higher elsewhere.

Front: The proposal was rejected because it was not in the public interest.
Meaning: Done to benefit society or the community as a whole.
Examples: The committee delayed permits in the public interest until safety tests were complete. | The journalist asked for the document in the public interest, not for political gain. | The city widened sidewalks in the public interest after several incidents.

Front: I changed the layout on the fly when the meeting room projector failed.
Meaning: Improvised or adjusted quickly while work is already in progress.
Examples: The trainer improvised vocabulary on the fly when students asked new questions. | We fixed the chart on the fly during the demo when the numbers changed. | Her code worked on the fly because she wrote a fallback path during testing.

Front: The dashboard updates in real time, so the support team can react immediately.
Meaning: Available as it happens, without delay.
Examples: The moderator monitored comments in real time and removed harmful posts quickly. | A translator app in real time helped with the client call. | The dashboard tracks delivery delays in real time during peak traffic.

Front: In this line of work, trust is built through reliable delivery and clear communication.
Meaning: Common expectation or standard within a specific profession.
Examples: Editors in this line of work often refuse to work without a second proofread. | In this line of work, missing a deadline usually means restarting the client conversation. | New developers in this line of work learn incident escalation before they touch production.

Front: That camera is top of the line for low-light photography.
Meaning: Among the highest quality or most advanced options available.
Examples: The hospital bought top-of-the-line scanners for the new wing. | This laptop is top of the line, but it may be more than we need. | Their top-of-the-line plan includes priority support.

Front: That contradiction is at the heart of the hiring debate, not a minor issue.
Meaning: The central cause or main point behind a larger matter.
Examples: The issue at the heart of the merger was data ownership. | Trust is at the heart of any long-term partnership. | Her argument missed the point because she ignored what was at the heart of the complaint.

Front: If you launch at the right time, retention improves naturally.
Meaning: When the timing is suitable and likely to be effective.
Examples: The chef moved the interview to a quieter hour at the right time. | We sent the invoice at the right time, before the budget window closed. | A small reminder at the right time can prevent most support tickets.

Front: The backup link is in the right place where new users can find it.
Meaning: Located where it is useful, expected, or appropriate.
Examples: Keep the first-aid kit in the right place near the exit. | The file was in the right place in the shared drive. | A reminder in the right place can prevent many support tickets.

Front: The topic of delayed reimbursement is back on the table for this quarter.
Meaning: A subject is currently being discussed and considered.
Examples: The idea of remote work came back on the table after the incident. | Salary bands are on the table, but the dates are not. | The contractor kept the delivery issue on the table until it was resolved.

Front: real progress can only be verified on the ground, not in the slide deck.
Meaning: In real-world, practical conditions rather than theory.
Examples: The NGO trained teams on the ground before shipping supplies. | Policy looked good, but on the ground the equipment was arriving broken. | We verified the service availability on the ground using two field agents.

Front: A new subsidy could be on the horizon if the vote passes next week.
Meaning: Likely to happen soon or be expected in the near future.
Examples: A major update is on the horizon for the mobile app. | The storm moved on the horizon before the city issued an alert. | New pricing was on the horizon after the pilot results came in.

Front: The mentor is available on demand when your study group gets stuck.
Meaning: Whenever needed, immediately requested, not scheduled.
Examples: The server scales on demand when user traffic spikes. | The driver came on demand during the weekend shift. | Our cloud scripts can generate report snapshots on demand for the finance team.

Front: The invoice is on hold until accounting confirms the tax number.
Meaning: Temporarily paused or waiting for another action.
Examples: The campaign is on hold while legal reviews the copy. | We kept the account on hold after suspicious activity appeared. | New hires are on hold until background checks are cleared.

Front: In light of recent outages, we moved the release to next Monday.
Meaning: Considering a specific context or new information when making a decision.
Examples: In light of the weather forecast, we canceled the outdoor event. | The doctor prescribed a lighter schedule in light of her recent fatigue. | In light of last year's errors, we added a second review step.

Front: In contrast with our old workflow, this version is much faster.
Meaning: Used to highlight a comparison between two things.
Examples: In contrast with last quarter, churn dropped after we changed pricing. | Her report was upbeat, in contrast with the team's earlier concerns. | In contrast with theory, the client expected faster setup.

Front: I can in no way recommend that shortcut for security work.
Meaning: A strong way to deny or reject something completely.
Examples: We can in no way share this list outside the group. | The candidate was in no way prepared for a live demo. | This result in no way proves that the method is safe.

Front: The project passed quality checks without a doubt.
Meaning: With complete certainty and no hesitation.
Examples: The diagnosis was correct without a doubt after repeated testing. | Her fluency made the presentation sound without a doubt top-notch. | The team was without a doubt the only one with the data source needed.

Front: The memo is in effect starting tomorrow, including the updated reimbursement rules.
Meaning: Currently active and producing real-world effect.
Examples: The policy is in effect even though it was not yet in the handbook. | Once the patch was in effect, error rates dropped in all regions. | His apology was in effect a reset of the relationship, and they worked again.

Front: In practice, we review every pull request before merging.
Meaning: How something actually works when performed day-to-day.
Examples: The idea sounded good in theory, but in practice the training took longer. | She tested the app in practice across three screen sizes before release. | In practice, the receptionist handles most support follow-ups manually.

Front: The idea is in principle acceptable, though we still need details.
Meaning: Generally agreed in theory but not necessarily applied yet.
Examples: The policy is in principle fair, but the procedure is too slow. | She is in principle open to remote work, but final details are pending. | The plan is in principle approved, subject to budget confirmation.

Front: The invoice was paid in full before production started.
Meaning: Completely, with nothing missing or unpaid.
Examples: Please read the policy in full before signing. | The refund was issued in full after the cancellation. | She answered the question in full, including the legal details.

Front: He learned the reporting system in the process of shadowing me.
Meaning: During or as part of an ongoing sequence of actions.
Examples: The assistant picked up three new skills in the process of project onboarding. | We found the delay in the process when approvals stacked up. | She became a better presenter in the process of leading the weekly standup.

Front: By Friday, the kitchen renovation was in full swing.
Meaning: Operating with full activity or intensity.
Examples: The conference was in full swing by the time we arrived. | Hiring is in full swing now that the budget is approved. | The debate was in full swing before the keynote resumed.

Front: We should contact the warehouse in the first instance and escalate only if needed.
Meaning: At the initial step, before further actions.
Examples: I should mention this in the first instance to avoid confusion later. | The lab called the supplier in the first instance when the samples were delayed. | In the first instance, we assumed the issue was network lag.

Front: I noticed the issue in the course of a routine audit.
Meaning: During the progress of a process or over a period.
Examples: In the course of the workshop, everyone drafted a fallback plan. | We found the data mismatch in the course of monthly reporting. | The team rebuilt the workflow in the course of migrating servers.

Front: The mayor announced the changes in a bid to improve safety quickly.
Meaning: In an attempt to achieve a specific goal.
Examples: The library opened at dawn in a bid to cut crowding. | She switched platforms in a bid to reduce latency by 20 percent. | The team met twice in a bid to meet the holiday deadline.

Front: In this context, "urgent" means within one hour, not one day.
Meaning: The meaning depends on the current situation.
Examples: In this context, "urgent" should route to the incident channel first. | She used "friend" differently in this context than in a court setting. | In this context, we need written confirmation, not just a verbal note.

Front: In that context, the phrase sounded rude, so I adjusted my wording.
Meaning: In that specific setting, the meaning or tone is different.
Examples: In that context, a direct "no" was the polite choice. | The gesture was funny in one setting but offensive in that context. | He agreed to the discount in that context, not in corporate meetings.

Front: They paused the rollout in the interest of customer safety.
Meaning: Done because it benefits or protects the stated goal/person.
Examples: The bank delayed marketing in the interest of avoiding misinformation. | We moved seats in the interest of faster boarding for disabled passengers. | The editor kept the claim out in the interest of factual accuracy.

Front: In comparison, the green route is slower but significantly cheaper.
Meaning: When judging two options side by side on a general level.
Examples: In comparison, the old model used more battery but had better battery life. | In comparison with weekdays, weekends feel shorter when you travel far. | The new policy is strict; in comparison, the old one was lenient.

Front: In comparison to 2019, our response time is now half of what it was.
Meaning: Direct comparison between two specific items.
Examples: In comparison to electric bikes, scooters were easier to maintain in this city. | Her proposal is in comparison to mine more realistic but less bold. | In comparison to local data, this benchmark looks surprisingly stable.

Front: She argued in support of a lower deposit to increase enrollment.
Meaning: Expressed as backing or endorsement for a person/idea.
Examples: The union voted in support of remote medical visits. | A friend spoke in support of her during the review meeting. | The city council supported the park expansion in support of commuters.

Front: The team voted in favor of publishing the correction immediately.
Meaning: Expressing preference, agreement, or advocacy for something.
Examples: The parents voted in favor of adding the workshop to the schedule. | He stood in favor of the proposal after reading the numbers. | We chose the cheaper option in favor of saving cash this quarter.

Front: The group spoke in opposition to the draft amendment.
Meaning: Positioned against or disagreeing with a proposal.
Examples: Students organized in opposition to the parking redesign. | The committee is currently in opposition to the budget line item. | She held a press note in opposition to the rumor and clarified facts.

Front: Our team is in opposition to moving the meeting to next week.
Meaning: Being against a plan or idea.
Examples: The union was in opposition when the contract language first came out. | The board sat in opposition for a full five minutes before voting. | Public comments in opposition pushed the team to revise the rollout.

Front: In the face of delays, she kept the timeline transparent.

Front: The lead engineer nipped the bug in the bud before release.
Meaning: Stop a problem early before it becomes serious.
Examples: The teacher nipped the rumor in the bud with a clear announcement. | We should nip scope creep in the bud this week. | HR nipped the conflict in the bud before teams took sides.

Front: His explanation does not hold water once you check the timestamps.
Meaning: Not be convincing or logically valid.
Examples: The cost argument does not hold water after the discount is included. | Her theory held water until the lab results arrived. | That excuse will not hold water with the audit team.

Phrase: to paint yourself into a corner
Front: The company painted itself into a corner with impossible delivery promises.
Meaning: Create a situation with very few good options left.
Examples: He painted himself into a corner by rejecting every compromise. | The contract painted us into a corner on support hours. | Do not paint yourself into a corner with a public deadline.

Front: Let me play devil's advocate: what if demand drops next month?
Meaning: Argue the opposite side to test an idea.
Examples: She played devil's advocate to expose a hidden risk. | I am playing devil's advocate, not rejecting the proposal. | The CFO played devil's advocate during the budget review.

Front: Everyone needs to pull their weight during the migration.
Meaning: Do a fair share of the work.
Examples: He did not pull his weight on the group project. | The new hire pulled her weight from the first sprint. | A small team only works if each person pulls their weight.

Phrase: to put something on the back burner
Front: We put the redesign on the back burner until the audit ends.
Meaning: Postpone something without cancelling it completely.
Examples: The feature is on the back burner while we fix reliability. | She put the certification on the back burner during the move. | Do not put security training on the back burner again.

Front: I cannot put my finger on why the sentence sounds unnatural.
Meaning: Identify the exact reason or problem.
Examples: She could not put her finger on the source of the delay. | He put his finger on the weakness in the proposal. | I knew something was wrong, but I could not put my finger on it.

Front: The director put her foot down and refused another deadline change.
Meaning: Insist firmly that something must or must not happen.
Examples: Parents sometimes need to put their foot down about screen time. | The editor put his foot down over factual errors. | She put her foot down when the vendor asked for a third extension.

Front: Read the room before making jokes in a tense meeting.
Meaning: Notice the mood and react appropriately.
Examples: He failed to read the room and pitched a celebration after layoffs. | A good speaker reads the room and adjusts pace. | She read the room and kept her comments brief.

Front: The proposal runs counter to our accessibility principles.
Meaning: Conflict with or go against something.
Examples: The decision ran counter to the evidence in the report. | His advice runs counter to standard security practice. | That tone runs counter to the brand voice.

Front: The CEO released a statement to set the record straight.
Meaning: Correct false or misleading information publicly.
Examples: She set the record straight about who approved the budget. | The report sets the record straight on the timeline. | He wanted to set the record straight before rumors spread.

Front: The article only skims the surface of the housing crisis.
Meaning: Discuss something very briefly without depth.
Examples: The workshop skimmed the surface of machine learning ethics. | We only skimmed the surface in the first meeting. | His answer skimmed the surface and ignored implementation risk.

Front: His silence spoke volumes during the salary discussion.
Meaning: Reveal a lot without explicit words.
Examples: The cancellation spoke volumes about internal confidence. | Her detailed questions spoke volumes about preparation. | The lack of documentation speaks volumes about the project culture.

Front: It stands to reason that fewer handoffs reduce mistakes.
Meaning: Be logical or expected from the facts.
Examples: It stands to reason that clearer instructions reduce support tickets. | If prices rise sharply, it stands to reason that demand may fall. | It stands to reason that faster feedback improves writing.

Front: The policy tries to strike a balance between speed and safety.
Meaning: Find a fair middle point between competing needs.
Examples: We need to strike a balance between automation and human review. | She struck a balance between confidence and caution. | The school is trying to strike a balance on phone use.

Front: Design polish took a back seat while we fixed data loss.
Meaning: Become less important than something else.
Examples: Marketing took a back seat during the compliance review. | His personal preferences took a back seat to user safety. | New features took a back seat until reliability improved.

Front: I take issue with the assumption that users read every warning.
Meaning: Disagree with something and consider it problematic.
Examples: She took issue with the report's conclusion, not the data. | Several reviewers took issue with the vague success metric. | I take issue with calling this a minor bug.

Front: A short walk took the edge off his anxiety before the call.
Meaning: Make something unpleasant feel less intense.
Examples: The quiet room took the edge off the long journey. | A small discount took the edge off the price increase. | The warm-up questions took the edge off the interview.

Front: New employees felt pressure to toe the line on public messaging.
Meaning: Follow rules or expectations closely, often reluctantly.
Examples: The spokesperson toed the line during the hostile interview. | He refused to toe the line when the numbers looked misleading. | The memo reminded managers to toe the line on compliance language.

Front: The supervisor turned a blind eye to repeated time-sheet errors.
Meaning: Deliberately ignore something wrong.
Examples: The school cannot turn a blind eye to bullying. | Regulators accused the firm of turning a blind eye to risk. | Do not turn a blind eye when the process is being bypassed.

Front: The spokesperson walked a fine line between honesty and panic.
Meaning: Manage a delicate balance where mistakes have consequences.
Examples: The article walks a fine line between criticism and speculation. | Teachers walk a fine line between support and pressure. | The negotiator walked a fine line during the final call.

Front: The company weathered the storm by cutting nonessential costs.
Meaning: Survive a difficult period.
Examples: The team weathered the storm after the public outage. | She weathered the storm of criticism and kept improving. | Small suppliers may struggle to weather the storm if demand drops.

Front: Weigh your words carefully before replying to that accusation.
Meaning: Choose language carefully because the situation is sensitive.
Examples: The diplomat weighed her words during the press conference. | He weighed his words before giving negative feedback. | In legal emails, weigh your words and avoid exaggeration.

Front: When push comes to shove, customers care more about reliability than design.
Meaning: When the situation becomes serious and a choice must be made.
Examples: When push comes to shove, the team will prioritize safety. | The policy sounds flexible, but when push comes to shove, budget wins. | When push comes to shove, she always protects her staff.

Front: He changed the deadline without so much as warning the team.
Meaning: Without even doing a small expected action.
Examples: The vendor cancelled without so much as an apology. | She left the meeting without so much as a goodbye. | They altered the contract without so much as asking legal.

Front: For what it's worth, the quietest users often give the clearest feedback.
Meaning: Used before offering an opinion that may or may not be useful.
Examples: For what it's worth, I think the simpler design will age better. | The numbers are early, but for what it's worth, retention is improving. | For what it's worth, she warned us about this risk last month.

Front: As luck would have it, the backup server failed during the demo.
Meaning: By chance, often in a surprising way.
Examples: As luck would have it, we found the missing receipt in an old email. | As luck would have it, the expert was already in the building. | The train was delayed, but as luck would have it, the meeting started late too.

Front: The proposal is risky, not least because the timeline depends on one vendor.
Meaning: Especially because; used to highlight an important reason.
Examples: The change matters, not least because customers already complained. | She was promoted, not least because she handled the crisis calmly. | The policy failed, not least because nobody explained it clearly.

Front: Be that as it may, we still need written approval before launch.
Meaning: Even if that is true, the main point remains different.
Examples: The tool is popular; be that as it may, it fails our security review. | He may be experienced; be that as it may, the rule applies to everyone. | The price is attractive; be that as it may, support is limited.
